name: Zeebrugge
summary: Zeebrugge is a busy fishing and cargo port on Belgium's North Sea coast where Dutch (Flemish) is spoken. Just 15km away sits Bruges, a fairy-tale medieval town crisscrossed by canals, and Brussels, Belgium's capital, is about 1.5 hours by train. This is the land of chocolate, crispy frites, waffles, and famous comic heroes like Tintin and the Smurfs.
funFacts: Bruges is nicknamed the Venice of the North because of its 12th-century canals, and its whole historic centre has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since the year 2000.
Belgium gave the world both Tintin (created in 1929) and the Smurfs (invented by artist Peyo in 1958) plus around 700 other comic-book characters.
The Atomium in Brussels is shaped like nine giant metal balls forming an iron crystal blown up 165 billion times, and stands a whopping 102 metres tall.
Belgians eat about 5.6 kilograms of chocolate per person each year, and Brussels Airport is the single biggest chocolate-selling spot on the entire planet.
Brussels' most famous statue is Manneken Pis, a tiny 55cm bronze boy peeing into a fountain who has over 1,000 costumes that are changed throughout the year.
thingsToDo: Hop on the Kusttram (Coast Tram) from near the cruise terminal to Blankenberge, then catch a quick train to Bruges for a perfect day trip.
Climb all 366 steep steps of the 83-metre Belfry of Bruges in the Markt square for a huge view across red rooftops and canals.
Glide through Bruges on a half-hour canal boat ride, slipping under tiny stone bridges past swans, towers and centuries-old merchant houses.
Sample real Belgian chocolate at a Bruges chocolatier like Chocolate Line or Dumon, then grab a cone of frites with mayo from a friterie stall.
Take the train from Bruges to Brussels to see the giant silver Atomium, the cheeky Manneken Pis statue, and Tintin murals on the city walls.
Visit Sea Life Blankenberge to walk the underwater shark tunnel and meet rescued harbour seals and Humboldt penguins, all one tram stop from the port.

images.alt: A pretty canal in Bruges with old brick houses and the tall Belfry tower behind them.
The huge Grand Place square in Brussels lined with tall, fancy gold-trimmed buildings.
The shiny silver Atomium in Brussels, made of giant metal balls connected by tubes.
The famous tiny Manneken Pis bronze fountain statue in Brussels.
A paper cone full of crispy golden Belgian fries ready to eat.
